etymology.description: semantic 勹 (wrap/enclose) + phonetic 凶 (xiōng, ominous) -> original meaning related to chest
etymology.interpretation: 匈 originally referred to the chest, combining the enclosing or wrapping notion of 勹 to suggest an external area containing vital organs, with 凶 contributing the sound. It was later extended to mean 'chest' in the context of the body, subsequently leading to the creation of 胸 (xiōng, 'chest') by adding 肉 (meat/flesh) for clarity.

examples.chinese: 他的匈部感到疼痛(他的匈部感到疼痛)。
医生检查了他的匈部(醫生檢查了他的匈部)。
examples.pinyin: Yīshēng jiǎnchá le tā de xiōngbù.

examples.chinese: 他用手捶打自己的匈口(他用手捶打自己的匈口)。
examples.pinyin: Tā yòng shǒu chuídǎ zìjǐ de xiōngkǒu.
examples.english: He hit his own chest with his hand.
